definition: An exact location in space, named by a capital letter, with no size or dimension.
example_1: Point A names one exact location — it has no length, width, or height.
example_2: Two points, A and B, can be connected to form a line segment.
key_rule: A point has zero dimensions — no length, width, or thickness.
memory_hook: Just a location — no size at all.

definition: A straight path of points that extends infinitely in both directions, with no thickness.
example_1: Line AB extends forever in both directions through points A and B.
example_2: Line AB and line BA name the same line.
key_rule: A line has no endpoints — it goes on forever both ways.
memory_hook: No start, no end, straight forever.

definition: A flat, two-dimensional surface that extends without end in every direction.
example_1: Three points that are not on the same line name exactly one plane, Plane P.
example_2: A tabletop extended infinitely in every direction represents a plane.
key_rule: A plane is perfectly flat and extends infinitely in all directions.
memory_hook: An endless flat sheet.

definition: A part of a line bounded by two endpoints, having a definite length.
example_1: Segment AB has two endpoints and a measurable length, such as AB = 6 cm.
example_2: A segment 10 cm long has two fixed endpoints, unlike a ray or line.
key_rule: Unlike a line, a segment has two endpoints and a fixed, measurable length.
memory_hook: A piece of a line with two ends.

definition: A part of a line with one endpoint that extends infinitely in one direction.
example_1: Ray AB starts at endpoint A, passes through B, and continues forever in one direction.
example_2: Ray BA would start at B and extend through A and beyond — a different ray than ray AB.
key_rule: A ray has exactly one endpoint and extends forever in only one direction.
memory_hook: One end fixed, the other end never stops.

definition: A figure formed by two rays sharing a common endpoint, called the vertex.
example_1: Angle ABC is formed by rays BA and BC meeting at vertex B.
example_2: The middle letter in a named angle, like ABC, is always the vertex.
key_rule: The middle letter of an angle's name always names the vertex.
memory_hook: Two rays, one shared corner.

definition: The common point where two rays of an angle, or two sides of a polygon, meet.
example_1: The vertex B is the common endpoint (corner) where the two rays of angle ABC meet.
example_2: A triangle has three vertices, one at each corner.
key_rule: The vertex is always the corner point where two rays or sides meet.
memory_hook: The corner point where things meet.

definition: The unit of angle measure; a full rotation is 360 degrees.
example_1: A right angle measures 90 degrees.
example_2: A full circle measures 360 degrees.
key_rule: Degrees measure how open or closed an angle is.
memory_hook: The unit for measuring how wide an angle opens.

definition: An angle that measures greater than 0 and less than 90 degrees.
example_1: 45 degrees is an acute angle.
example_2: 10 degrees is an acute angle.
key_rule: An acute angle is always smaller than a right angle.
memory_hook: A small, 'cute' angle — under 90 degrees.

definition: An angle that measures exactly 90 degrees, shown with a small square mark.
example_1: A right angle always measures exactly 90 degrees.
example_2: The corner of a square or rectangle is a right angle.
key_rule: A right angle is always marked with a small corner square, not an arc.
memory_hook: Exactly 90 degrees — marked with a little square.

definition: An angle that measures greater than 90 and less than 180 degrees.
example_1: 120 degrees is an obtuse angle.
example_2: 150 degrees is an obtuse angle.
key_rule: An obtuse angle is always wider than a right angle but not a straight line.
memory_hook: Wider than a right angle, but not flat.

definition: An angle that measures exactly 180 degrees, forming a straight line.
example_1: Points A, B, and C on a straight line form a 180 degree straight angle.
example_2: A linear pair of angles always adds up to a straight angle, 180 degrees.
key_rule: A straight angle looks like a straight line, not a bent corner.
memory_hook: A flat angle — exactly 180 degrees.

definition: The point that divides a segment into two congruent halves.
example_1: Midpoint M divides segment AB so that AM = MB.
example_2: The midpoint of a 10 cm segment is 5 cm from each end.
key_rule: A midpoint always creates two congruent (equal-length) halves.
memory_hook: The exact halfway point.

definition: A line, ray, or segment that divides another segment into two equal parts at its midpoint.
example_1: A segment bisector passes through midpoint M, cutting the segment into two equal halves.
example_2: Any line through a segment's midpoint is a segment bisector of that segment.
key_rule: A segment bisector always passes through the midpoint.
memory_hook: Cuts a segment exactly in half.

definition: A ray that divides an angle into two congruent (equal) angles.
example_1: An angle bisector splits an 80 degree angle into two 40 degree angles.
example_2: An angle bisector of a 60 degree angle creates two 30 degree angles.
key_rule: An angle bisector always creates two congruent angles.
memory_hook: Cuts an angle exactly in half.

definition: Segments that have equal length, indicated by matching tick marks.
example_1: If AB = CD, the segments are congruent, shown with matching tick marks.
example_2: Two 6 cm segments are congruent because they have equal length.
key_rule: Matching tick marks on a diagram show which segments are congruent.
memory_hook: Same length, matching tick marks.

definition: Angles that have equal measure, indicated by matching arc marks.
example_1: If angle 1 = angle 2, the angles are congruent, shown with matching arc marks.
example_2: Two 50 degree angles are congruent because they have equal measure.
key_rule: Matching arc marks on a diagram show which angles are congruent.
memory_hook: Same measure, matching arc marks.

definition: Two angles whose measures have a sum of 90 degrees.
example_1: 60 degrees and 30 degrees are complementary because 60 + 30 = 90.
example_2: 20 degrees and 70 degrees are complementary because 20 + 70 = 90.
key_rule: Complementary angles always add up to exactly 90 degrees.
memory_hook: 'C' for complementary, 'c' for corner — they add to 90 degrees.

definition: Two angles whose measures have a sum of 180 degrees.
example_1: 110 degrees and 70 degrees are supplementary because 110 + 70 = 180.
example_2: 150 degrees and 30 degrees are supplementary because 150 + 30 = 180.
key_rule: Supplementary angles always add up to exactly 180 degrees.
memory_hook: 'S' for supplementary, 's' for straight — they add to 180 degrees.

definition: The pair of opposite, congruent angles formed when two lines intersect.
example_1: When two lines cross, the opposite angles are vertical angles and always equal, such as 50 = 50 degrees.
example_2: Vertical angles are never adjacent — they sit directly across from each other.
key_rule: Vertical angles are always congruent.
memory_hook: Across the X, the angles match.

definition: Two angles that share a common vertex and side but have no interior points in common.
example_1: Angle 1 and angle 2 share a vertex and a side without overlapping.
example_2: Two angles that together form a straight line are adjacent angles.
key_rule: Adjacent angles share a vertex and a side but never overlap.
memory_hook: Next-door angles — sharing a side, not overlapping.

definition: Two adjacent angles that form a straight line, so their measures sum to 180 degrees.
example_1: A linear pair of 130 and 50 degrees sums to 180 degrees.
example_2: Any two adjacent angles whose non-shared sides form a straight line are a linear pair.
key_rule: A linear pair is always supplementary — the angles sum to 180 degrees.
memory_hook: Adjacent angles that make a straight line.

definition: Two lines, rays, or segments that intersect to form right angles.
example_1: Perpendicular lines meet at a right angle (90 degrees), written AB perpendicular to CD.
example_2: The two sides of a square meet perpendicular to each other.
key_rule: Perpendicular lines always cross at exactly 90 degrees.
memory_hook: Crosses like a plus sign — always at 90 degrees.

definition: Lines in the same plane that never intersect and stay an equal distance apart.
example_1: Line m and line n are parallel, written m parallel to n.
example_2: Railroad tracks are a real-world example of parallel lines.
key_rule: Parallel lines are marked with matching arrowheads and never meet.
memory_hook: Same distance apart, forever — never touching.

definition: A line that intersects two or more coplanar lines at distinct points.
example_1: Transversal t crosses two parallel lines, creating 8 angles at the two intersections.
example_2: A transversal crossing two lines creates pairs like corresponding and alternate interior angles.
key_rule: A transversal creates eight angles when it crosses two lines.
memory_hook: The line that cuts across two others.

definition: Angles in matching positions at two intersections of a transversal; congruent when the lines are parallel.
example_1: 70 = 70 degrees for a pair of corresponding angles on parallel lines.
example_2: The top-left angle at each intersection is a pair of corresponding angles.
key_rule: Corresponding angles are congruent only when the two lines are parallel.
memory_hook: Same position at each crossing.

definition: Congruent angles between two parallel lines on opposite sides of the transversal.
example_1: 65 = 65 degrees for a pair of alternate interior angles on parallel lines.
example_2: Alternate interior angles form a 'Z' pattern between the two lines.
key_rule: Alternate interior angles are congruent only when the lines are parallel.
memory_hook: Inside the lines, opposite sides — the 'Z' pattern.

definition: Congruent angles outside two parallel lines on opposite sides of the transversal.
example_1: 115 = 115 degrees for a pair of alternate exterior angles on parallel lines.
example_2: Alternate exterior angles sit above and below both parallel lines, on opposite sides of the transversal.
key_rule: Alternate exterior angles are congruent only when the lines are parallel.
memory_hook: Outside the lines, opposite sides.

definition: Same-side interior angles between two parallel lines that sum to 180 degrees.
example_1: 120 + 60 = 180 degrees for a pair of co-interior angles on parallel lines.
example_2: Co-interior angles sit between the lines, on the same side of the transversal.
key_rule: Co-interior angles are supplementary when the lines are parallel.
memory_hook: Same side, inside — they add to 180 degrees.

definition: Two lines that intersect to form right angles.
example_1: Perpendicular lines intersect at 90 degrees, forming four right angles, written a perpendicular to b.
example_2: The x-axis and y-axis are perpendicular lines.
key_rule: Perpendicular lines always create four 90 degree angles at their intersection.
memory_hook: Crossing lines that make right angles.

definition: A triangle with all three sides of different lengths.
example_1: A triangle with sides 5, 7, and 9 is scalene — no two sides match.
example_2: A scalene triangle also has no two equal angles.
key_rule: A scalene triangle has no congruent sides and no congruent angles.
memory_hook: All different — no matching sides.

definition: A triangle with at least two congruent sides and two congruent base angles.
example_1: A triangle with two 7-unit sides and a 5-unit base is isosceles.
example_2: The two base angles across from the equal sides are always congruent.
key_rule: An isosceles triangle's base angles are always congruent.
memory_hook: At least two sides match, and so do the base angles.

definition: A triangle with three congruent sides and three 60 degree angles.
example_1: An equilateral triangle has three 60 degree angles: 60 x 3 = 180.
example_2: All three sides of an equilateral triangle are equal in length.
key_rule: Every equilateral triangle is also isosceles, but not every isosceles triangle is equilateral.
memory_hook: All equal — sides and angles alike.

definition: The interior angles of any triangle always add up to 180 degrees.
example_2: In an equilateral triangle, each angle is 60, so 60 + 60 + 60 = 180.
key_rule: The three interior angles of any triangle sum to exactly 180 degrees.
memory_hook: Every triangle's angles add to 180 degrees.

definition: An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles.
example_1: 50 + 60 = 110 degrees, so the exterior angle measures 110 degrees.
example_2: If the remote interior angles are 40 and 75 degrees, the exterior angle is 115 degrees.
key_rule: An exterior angle always equals the sum of the two remote interior angles.
memory_hook: The outside angle equals the two far-away inside angles added up.

definition: Triangles are congruent if all three pairs of corresponding sides are equal.
example_1: If three sides of one triangle equal three sides of another, the triangles are congruent by SSS.
example_2: Triangles with sides 3-4-5 and 3-4-5 are congruent by SSS.
key_rule: SSS needs all three side pairs to match — no angles required.
memory_hook: Side, Side, Side — three matching sides prove it.

definition: Triangles are congruent if two sides and the angle between them are equal.
example_1: Two sides and the included angle matching proves triangles congruent by SAS.
example_2: Triangles with sides 5 and 7 and an included 40 degree angle, matched in both triangles, are congruent by SAS.
key_rule: The angle in SAS must be the included angle — the one between the two given sides.
memory_hook: Side, Angle, Side — the angle must be sandwiched between the sides.

definition: Triangles are congruent if two angles and the side between them are equal.
example_1: Two angles and the included side matching proves triangles congruent by ASA.
example_2: Triangles with 40 and 60 degree angles and a matching included side are congruent by ASA.
key_rule: The side in ASA must be the included side — the one between the two given angles.
memory_hook: Angle, Side, Angle — the side must be sandwiched between the angles.

definition: Triangles are congruent if two angles and a non-included side are equal.
example_1: Two angles and a side that is NOT between them, matched in both triangles, proves congruence by AAS.
example_2: Triangles with 30 and 70 degree angles and a matching non-included side are congruent by AAS.
key_rule: AAS uses a side that is not between the two given angles.
memory_hook: Angle, Angle, Side — the side sits outside the two angles.

definition: Right triangles are congruent if their hypotenuses and one pair of legs are equal.
example_1: Two right triangles with equal hypotenuses and one equal leg are congruent by HL.
example_2: HL only applies to right triangles, since it relies on the hypotenuse.
key_rule: HL only works for right triangles.
memory_hook: Hypotenuse and one Leg — right triangles only.

definition: Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent, used after a congruence proof.
example_1: Once two triangles are proven congruent, every corresponding side and angle is congruent.
example_2: After proving triangle ABC is congruent to triangle DEF, CPCTC lets you conclude angle A is congruent to angle D.
key_rule: CPCTC only applies after congruence has already been proven, such as by SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL.
memory_hook: Once congruent, every matching part is congruent.

definition: A segment from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side.
example_1: A median joins vertex A to midpoint M of the opposite side BC.
example_2: Every triangle has exactly three medians, one from each vertex.
key_rule: A median always connects a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side.
memory_hook: Vertex to the midpoint of the far side.

definition: A perpendicular segment from a vertex to the line containing the opposite side.
example_1: The altitude from vertex A is perpendicular to side BC — it's the triangle's height.
example_2: Every triangle has three altitudes, one from each vertex.
key_rule: An altitude always meets the opposite side at a right angle.
memory_hook: The triangle's height — straight down, at 90 degrees.

definition: A segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle, parallel to the third side and half its length.
example_1: A midsegment is half the length of the base it's parallel to.
example_2: If the base of a triangle is 10 units, its midsegment is 5 units.
key_rule: A midsegment is always parallel to the third side and exactly half its length.
memory_hook: Half the length, parallel to the far side.

definition: The point of intersection of a triangle's three medians, also its center of gravity.
example_1: The centroid splits each median in a 2:1 ratio from the vertex.
example_2: A triangle balances perfectly if supported at its centroid.
key_rule: The centroid always splits each median 2:1, measured from the vertex.
memory_hook: Where the medians meet — the balance point.

definition: The point where a triangle's three perpendicular bisectors meet, equidistant from the vertices.
example_1: The circumcenter is the center of the circle that passes through all three vertices.
example_2: Distances from the circumcenter to each vertex are always equal.
key_rule: The circumcenter is equidistant from all three vertices of the triangle.
memory_hook: Where the perpendicular bisectors cross — center of the circumscribed circle.

definition: Figures with the same shape, having congruent angles and proportional side lengths.
example_1: Triangle ABC ~ Triangle DEF with side ratios 6:3 = 8:4 = 10:5 = 2, so the figures are similar.
example_2: Two rectangles are similar if their side lengths share the same ratio, like 4:2 and 8:4.
key_rule: Similar figures always have congruent angles and proportional sides.
memory_hook: Same shape, different size — angles match, sides scale.

definition: The ratio of corresponding lengths in similar figures, used to enlarge or reduce.
example_1: If a figure's sides double, the scale factor is 2.
example_2: A scale factor of 1/2 shrinks a figure to half its original size.
key_rule: A scale factor greater than 1 enlarges; less than 1 reduces.
memory_hook: The multiplier that resizes similar figures.

definition: Two triangles are similar if two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent.
example_1: If two angles of one triangle equal two angles of another, the triangles are similar by AA.
example_2: Matching 40 and 60 degree angles in two triangles proves similarity by AA.
key_rule: Only two angle pairs are needed to prove triangle similarity — the third angle pair follows automatically.
memory_hook: Two matching angles is enough — the third takes care of itself.

definition: In a right triangle, a squared plus b squared equals c squared, where c is the hypotenuse.
example_1: 3 squared + 4 squared = 9 + 16 = 25 = 5 squared, so c = 5.
example_2: 6 squared + 8 squared = 36 + 64 = 100 = 10 squared, so c = 10.
key_rule: The Pythagorean theorem only works for right triangles, and c is always the hypotenuse.
memory_hook: a squared plus b squared equals c squared, right triangles only.

definition: The side of a right triangle opposite the right angle; it is the longest side.
example_1: In a 3-4-5 right triangle, the side of length 5 is the hypotenuse.
example_2: The hypotenuse is always across from the 90 degree angle, never adjacent to it.
key_rule: The hypotenuse is always the longest side of a right triangle.
memory_hook: The longest side — always across from the right angle.

definition: One of the two sides of a right triangle that form the right angle.
example_1: In a 3-4-5 right triangle, the sides of length 3 and 4 are the legs.
example_2: The two legs meet at the right angle, forming the 90 degree corner.
key_rule: A right triangle has exactly two legs and one hypotenuse.
memory_hook: The two shorter sides that form the right angle.

definition: An isosceles right triangle whose hypotenuse equals a leg times the square root of 2.
example_1: If each leg is 1, the hypotenuse is the square root of 2, giving a side ratio of 1 : 1 : root 2.
example_2: If each leg is 5, the hypotenuse is 5 times the square root of 2.
key_rule: In a 45-45-90 triangle, the two legs are always equal.
memory_hook: Equal legs, hypotenuse is a leg times the square root of 2.

definition: A right triangle whose sides are in the ratio x : x times root 3 : 2x.
example_1: Sides opposite the 30, 60, and 90 degree angles are in ratio x : x root 3 : 2x.
example_2: If the shortest side (opposite 30 degrees) is 4, the sides are 4, 4 root 3, and 8.
key_rule: The side opposite the 30 degree angle is always the shortest, and the hypotenuse is always twice that side.
memory_hook: Short side x, middle side x root 3, hypotenuse 2x.

definition: In a right triangle, the ratio of the side opposite an angle to the hypotenuse.
example_1: sin(theta) = opposite over hypotenuse (SOH).
example_2: If the opposite side is 3 and the hypotenuse is 5, sin(theta) = 3/5 = 0.6.
key_rule: Sine always compares the opposite side to the hypotenuse.
memory_hook: SOH — Sine is Opposite over Hypotenuse.

definition: In a right triangle, the ratio of the side adjacent to an angle to the hypotenuse.
example_1: cos(theta) = adjacent over hypotenuse (CAH).
example_2: If the adjacent side is 4 and the hypotenuse is 5, cos(theta) = 4/5 = 0.8.
key_rule: Cosine always compares the adjacent side to the hypotenuse.
memory_hook: CAH — Cosine is Adjacent over Hypotenuse.

definition: In a right triangle, the ratio of the side opposite an angle to the side adjacent.
example_1: tan(theta) = opposite over adjacent (TOA).
example_2: If the opposite side is 3 and the adjacent side is 4, tan(theta) = 3/4 = 0.75.
key_rule: Tangent always compares the opposite side to the adjacent side, not the hypotenuse.
memory_hook: TOA — Tangent is Opposite over Adjacent.

definition: The angle measured upward from a horizontal line to an object above.
example_1: The angle of elevation to the top of a building is measured from eye level looking up.
example_2: A kite string making a 35 degree angle above the horizontal has a 35 degree angle of elevation.
key_rule: Angle of elevation is always measured from the horizontal, looking up.
memory_hook: Look up from level ground to measure it.

definition: A quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel and congruent.
example_1: In parallelogram ABCD, side AB is parallel and congruent to side DC.
example_2: Opposite angles in a parallelogram are also congruent.
key_rule: A parallelogram always has two pairs of parallel, congruent opposite sides.
memory_hook: Opposite sides parallel and equal.

definition: A parallelogram with four right angles and congruent diagonals.
example_1: A rectangle's diagonals are always equal in length.
example_2: Every rectangle is a parallelogram, but not every parallelogram is a rectangle.
key_rule: A rectangle is a parallelogram with four 90 degree angles.
memory_hook: A parallelogram with all right angles.

definition: A parallelogram with four congruent sides and perpendicular diagonals.
example_1: All four sides of a rhombus are equal in length.
example_2: A rhombus's diagonals always cross at a right angle.
key_rule: A rhombus is a parallelogram with all four sides equal.
memory_hook: A parallelogram with all equal sides.

definition: A regular quadrilateral with four congruent sides and four right angles.
example_1: A square has four equal sides and four right angles — it is both a rectangle and a rhombus.
example_2: Every square is a parallelogram, rectangle, and rhombus at once.
key_rule: A square combines the properties of both a rectangle and a rhombus.
memory_hook: Equal sides plus right angles — both a rectangle and a rhombus.

definition: A quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides.
example_1: The two parallel sides of a trapezoid are called its bases.
example_2: A trapezoid's non-parallel sides are called its legs.
key_rule: A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides, not two.
memory_hook: Only one pair of sides runs parallel.

definition: A quadrilateral with two distinct pairs of consecutive congruent sides and perpendicular diagonals.
example_1: A kite has two pairs of adjacent (consecutive) congruent sides.
example_2: A kite's diagonals always intersect at a right angle.
key_rule: In a kite, the congruent sides are adjacent (next to each other), not opposite.
memory_hook: Adjacent sides match, like a kite's frame.

definition: A closed two-dimensional figure formed by three or more straight sides.
example_1: A pentagon (5 sides), hexagon (6 sides), and octagon (8 sides) are all polygons.
example_2: A polygon must be a closed figure — every side must connect.
key_rule: A polygon is made only of straight line segments, with no gaps or curves.
memory_hook: Closed shape, straight sides.

definition: A polygon with all sides congruent and all angles congruent.
example_1: A regular polygon is equilateral (all sides equal) and equiangular (all angles equal).
example_2: A square is a regular polygon; a rectangle that isn't a square is not.
key_rule: A regular polygon must be both equilateral and equiangular.
memory_hook: All sides equal, all angles equal.

definition: The total of a polygon's interior angles, equal to (n - 2) times 180 degrees.
example_1: A pentagon (5 sides): (5 - 2) x 180 = 540 degrees.
example_2: A hexagon (6 sides): (6 - 2) x 180 = 720 degrees.
key_rule: The interior angle sum depends only on the number of sides, n.
memory_hook: (n - 2) x 180 degrees — subtract 2, multiply by 180.

definition: The exterior angles of any convex polygon total 360 degrees.
example_1: One exterior angle per vertex, and they always sum to 360 degrees, no matter how many sides.
example_2: A regular hexagon's six exterior angles are each 60 degrees, summing to 360 degrees.
key_rule: Exterior angle sum is always 360 degrees, regardless of the number of sides.
memory_hook: Always 360 degrees — no matter how many sides.

definition: A segment that joins two non-adjacent vertices of a polygon.
example_1: In quadrilateral ABCD, a diagonal connects A to C, skipping the adjacent vertices.
example_2: A rectangle has two diagonals, and they are always congruent.
key_rule: A diagonal connects vertices that are not next to each other.
memory_hook: Skips a corner to connect across the shape.

definition: A transformation that slides every point of a figure the same distance in the same direction.
example_1: Sliding triangle ABC to triangle A'B'C' without turning or flipping it is a translation.
example_2: Moving a figure 3 units right and 2 units up is a translation.
key_rule: A translation keeps the figure's size, shape, and orientation the same.
memory_hook: Slide it — same size, same direction, no turning.

definition: A transformation that flips a figure over a line, creating a mirror image.
example_1: Reflecting triangle ABC over a line produces triangle A'B'C', a mirror image.
example_2: Flipping a figure over the y-axis reverses its left-right orientation.
key_rule: A reflection always flips a figure over a line, reversing its orientation.
memory_hook: Flip it over the line — mirror image.

definition: A transformation that turns a figure about a fixed point by a given angle.
example_1: Turning a figure 90 degrees about center point C is a rotation.
example_2: A full rotation of 360 degrees returns a figure to its starting position.
key_rule: A rotation is defined by its center point, angle, and direction.
memory_hook: Turn it around a fixed point.

definition: A transformation that resizes a figure by a scale factor from a fixed center point.
example_1: A dilation with scale factor k = 2 doubles the size of the figure.
example_2: A dilation with scale factor 1/2 shrinks the figure to half its size.
key_rule: A dilation changes a figure's size but keeps it similar to the original.
memory_hook: Resize from a center point — shape stays the same, size changes.

definition: A transformation (slide, flip, or turn) that preserves size and shape, producing a congruent image.
example_1: Translations, reflections, and rotations are all rigid motions.
example_2: A rigid motion never changes a figure's distances or angles.
key_rule: Rigid motions always preserve distance and angle, so the image is congruent to the original.
memory_hook: Slide, flip, or turn — size and shape never change.

definition: The pre-image is the original figure, the image is its position after a transformation.
example_1: The pre-image is labeled ABC; the image after transformation is labeled A'B'C', with primes.
example_2: If the pre-image is a triangle at (0,0), (2,0), (0,3), the image after a translation shifts every point the same way.
key_rule: The image is always labeled with prime marks to distinguish it from the pre-image.
memory_hook: Original is the pre-image; after the move, it's the image — with primes.

definition: A line that divides a figure into two halves that are mirror images.
example_1: Folding a figure along its line of symmetry makes the two halves match exactly.
example_2: A square has four lines of symmetry.
key_rule: A figure's two halves must match exactly when folded along the line of symmetry.
memory_hook: Fold along it — the halves match perfectly.

definition: A figure has it when a rotation of less than a full turn maps the figure onto itself.
example_1: A regular pentagon has rotational symmetry — a 72 degree turn maps it onto itself.
example_2: A square has rotational symmetry at 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
key_rule: The rotation must be less than 360 degrees and still map the figure onto itself.
memory_hook: Turns before a full circle, but still looks the same.

definition: Performing two or more transformations in order, one after the other.
example_1: Reflecting a figure, then translating the result, is a composition of transformations.
example_2: A -> A' -> A'' shows two transformations applied in sequence.
key_rule: The order of transformations in a composition matters — doing them in a different order can give a different result.
memory_hook: One transformation, then another, in order.

definition: The fixed point about which a figure is rotated.
example_1: A figure turning around a fixed point C uses C as its center of rotation.
example_2: Every point of a rotated figure stays the same distance from the center of rotation.
key_rule: The center of rotation is the one point that does not move during a rotation.
memory_hook: The fixed pivot point a figure turns around.

definition: A segment connecting the center of a circle to a point on the circle.
example_1: Every radius of the same circle has the same length.
example_2: A circle with radius 5 has every point exactly 5 units from the center.
key_rule: A radius is always half the length of the diameter.
memory_hook: Center to edge.

definition: A segment through the center of a circle with both endpoints on the circle, equal to twice the radius.
example_1: d = 2r, so a circle with radius 4 has a diameter of 8.
example_2: A diameter is the longest chord that can be drawn in a circle.
key_rule: A diameter always passes through the center.
memory_hook: Straight through the center — twice the radius.

definition: A segment with both endpoints on a circle; the diameter is the longest chord.
example_1: Any segment connecting two points on the circle's edge is a chord.
example_2: A diameter is a special chord that passes through the center.
key_rule: Every diameter is a chord, but not every chord is a diameter.
memory_hook: Endpoints on the circle — the diameter is just the longest one.

definition: A line that intersects a circle at exactly two points.
example_1: A secant line crosses through the circle, entering and exiting at two points.
example_2: Extending a chord into a full line makes it a secant.
key_rule: A secant crosses the circle, unlike a tangent, which only touches it once.
memory_hook: Cuts through — touches the circle twice.

definition: A line that touches a circle at exactly one point, perpendicular to the radius at that point.
example_1: A tangent line touches the circle at one point and never crosses into it.
example_2: The radius drawn to the point of tangency is always perpendicular to the tangent line.
key_rule: A tangent line always meets the radius at a 90 degree angle at the point of contact.
memory_hook: Touches once, perpendicular to the radius there.

definition: An angle whose vertex is the center of the circle; it equals its intercepted arc.
example_1: A central angle of 80 degrees corresponds to an 80 degree arc.
example_2: A central angle of 45 degrees cuts off a 45 degree arc.
key_rule: A central angle's measure always equals the measure of its intercepted arc.
memory_hook: Vertex at the center — angle equals its arc.

definition: An angle with its vertex on the circle; it measures half its intercepted arc.
example_1: An inscribed angle of 40 degrees corresponds to an intercepted arc of 80 degrees.
example_2: If the intercepted arc is 100 degrees, the inscribed angle measures 50 degrees.
key_rule: An inscribed angle is always half the measure of its intercepted arc.
memory_hook: Vertex on the circle — angle is half its arc.

definition: A connected portion of the circumference of a circle.
example_1: Arc AB is the part of the circle's edge between points A and B.
example_2: A semicircle is an arc that measures exactly 180 degrees.
key_rule: An arc is always measured in degrees, matching its central angle.
memory_hook: A slice of the circle's outside edge.

definition: The minor arc is the shorter arc (under 180 degrees), the major arc the longer (over 180 degrees).
example_1: A minor arc measures less than 180 degrees, such as 80 degrees.
example_2: A major arc measures more than 180 degrees, such as 280 degrees.
key_rule: Together, a minor and major arc from the same two points always add up to 360 degrees.
memory_hook: Minor is the short way around, major is the long way.

definition: The distance along an arc, equal to (central angle / 360) times the circumference.
example_1: A 90 degree arc on a circle covers 90/360, or 1/4, of the circumference.
example_2: A 180 degree arc covers half the circumference.
key_rule: Arc length is the fraction of the circumference cut off by the central angle.
memory_hook: Fraction of the angle times the whole circumference.

definition: A region of a circle bounded by two radii and their intercepted arc.
example_1: A sector looks like a pie slice, bounded by two radii and the arc between them.
example_2: A quarter-circle sector has a central angle of 90 degrees.
key_rule: A sector is bounded by two radii and the arc they cut off — a 'pie slice' of the circle.
memory_hook: A pie-slice piece of the circle.

definition: (x - h) squared + (y - k) squared = r squared, with center (h, k) and radius r.
example_1: A circle centered at (2, 3) with radius 5 has the equation (x - 2) squared + (y - 3) squared = 25.
example_2: A circle centered at the origin with radius 4 has the equation x squared + y squared = 16.
key_rule: The center's coordinates are subtracted from x and y, and the right side is the radius squared.
memory_hook: (x - h) squared + (y - k) squared = r squared — center (h, k), radius r.

definition: d = square root of (x2 - x1) squared plus (y2 - y1) squared, the distance between two points.
example_1: From (0,0) to (3,4): d = square root of (9 + 16) = square root of 25 = 5.
example_2: From (1,1) to (4,5): d = square root of (9 + 16) = square root of 25 = 5.
key_rule: The distance formula comes directly from the Pythagorean theorem.
memory_hook: It's the Pythagorean theorem, applied to two coordinate points.

definition: The midpoint of a segment is ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2).
example_1: The midpoint of (2,4) and (6,8) is (4, 6).
example_2: The midpoint of (0,0) and (8,10) is (4, 5).
key_rule: The midpoint formula averages the x-coordinates and the y-coordinates separately.
memory_hook: Average the x's, average the y's.

definition: The steepness of a line, m = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1), or rise over run.
example_1: A rise of 4 over a run of 2 gives a slope of m = 2.
example_2: A line through (0,0) and (3,6) has slope (6-0)/(3-0) = 2.
key_rule: Slope measures a line's steepness as the change in y over the change in x.
memory_hook: Rise over run.

definition: Parallel lines have the same slope and never intersect.
example_1: Two non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if they have equal slopes, m1 = m2.
example_2: A line with slope 3 is parallel to any other line with slope 3.
key_rule: Equal slopes always mean two non-vertical lines are parallel.
memory_hook: Same slope, lines that never meet.

definition: Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.
example_1: Slopes of 2 and -1/2 are negative reciprocals, so the lines are perpendicular.
example_2: Slopes of 3 and -1/3 are negative reciprocals, so the lines are perpendicular.
key_rule: Two slopes multiply to -1 exactly when the lines are perpendicular.
memory_hook: Flip and negate the slope to find the perpendicular one.

definition: To find a point that divides a segment into a given ratio of lengths.
example_1: To partition segment AB in a 2:1 ratio, move 2/3 of the run and rise from point A.
example_2: Partitioning a segment in a 1:1 ratio gives the midpoint.
key_rule: Move the given fraction of the total run and rise, starting from the first point.
memory_hook: Move a fraction of the way along, based on the ratio.

definition: The amount of space inside a two-dimensional figure, measured in square units.
example_1: A rectangle 5 by 3 has an area of 5 x 3 = 15 square units.
example_2: A square with side 6 has an area of 36 square units.
key_rule: Area is always measured in square units because it covers two dimensions.
memory_hook: The surface a 2D figure covers.

definition: The total distance around the outside of a two-dimensional figure.
example_1: A rectangle with sides 8, 5, 8, 5 has a perimeter of 8+5+8+5 = 26 units.
example_2: A square with side 6 has a perimeter of 24 units.
key_rule: Perimeter is found by adding all the side lengths.
memory_hook: Walk the outside edge and add it up.

definition: The total area of all the surfaces (faces) of a three-dimensional figure.
example_1: Surface area is the sum of the areas of all the outer faces of a solid, in square units.
example_2: A cube with side 4 has 6 faces of 16 square units each, for a surface area of 96 square units.
key_rule: Add the areas of every outer face to find surface area.
memory_hook: Add up the area of every face.

definition: The space inside a prism, equal to the base area times the height, in cubic units.
example_1: A box 4 by 3 by 2: V = base area x height = 12 x 2 = 24 cubic units.
example_2: A triangular prism with base area 10 and height 6 has volume 60 cubic units.
key_rule: The volume of a prism equals the area of its base times its height.
memory_hook: Base area times height.

definition: V = pi r squared h, the circular base area times the height.
example_1: pi(3 squared)(5) = 45 pi cubic units.
example_2: A cylinder with radius 2 and height 10 has volume pi(2 squared)(10) = 40 pi cubic units.
key_rule: The volume of a cylinder equals the area of its circular base times its height.
memory_hook: Circle base area times height.

definition: V = one third pi r squared h, one third the cylinder of the same base and height.
example_1: A cone with the same base and height as a cylinder has exactly one third its volume.
example_2: A cone with radius 3 and height 6 has volume one third pi(3 squared)(6) = 18 pi cubic units.
key_rule: A cone's volume is always one third of a cylinder with the same base and height.
memory_hook: One third of a matching cylinder.

definition: V = one third B h, one third the base area times the height.
example_1: A pyramid with base area 12 and height 9 has volume one third (12)(9) = 36 cubic units.
example_2: A pyramid with the same base and height as a prism has exactly one third its volume.
key_rule: A pyramid's volume is always one third of a prism with the same base and height.
memory_hook: One third of a matching prism.

definition: V = four thirds pi r cubed, depending only on the radius.
example_1: Four thirds pi (3 cubed) = 36 pi cubic units.
example_2: A sphere with radius 6 has volume four thirds pi(6 cubed) = 288 pi cubic units.
key_rule: A sphere's volume depends only on its radius, cubed.
memory_hook: Four-thirds pi r cubed.

definition: The two-dimensional shape formed when a plane cuts through a three-dimensional figure.
example_1: A horizontal slice through a cylinder produces a circle as its cross-section.
example_2: A vertical slice through a rectangular prism produces a rectangle.
key_rule: The shape of a cross-section depends on the angle and direction of the slice.
memory_hook: The flat shape revealed by a slice.

definition: The total area of the side surfaces of a solid, excluding its bases.
example_1: Lateral area includes only the side surface, not the top or bottom base.
example_2: A cylinder's lateral area is the curved side, not the two circular ends.
key_rule: Lateral area excludes the base(s); surface area includes them.
memory_hook: Just the sides — no bases counted.

definition: A three-dimensional figure made of two or more simple solids combined.
example_1: A solid made of a cylinder and a cone: V = V(cylinder) + V(cone), adding the volumes of the parts.
example_2: A figure made of a rectangular prism topped with a pyramid finds its volume by adding both parts.
key_rule: Find a composite solid's volume by adding the volumes of its simple parts.
memory_hook: Break it into simple solids, then add up the volumes.

definition: The point where a triangle's three angle bisectors meet, equidistant from the sides.
example_1: The incenter I is equidistant from all three sides of triangle ABC — the radius of the inscribed circle.
example_2: The incenter is always inside the triangle, unlike the circumcenter, which can fall outside.
key_rule: The incenter is the center of the triangle's inscribed circle (incircle), tangent to all three sides.
memory_hook: Where the angle bisectors meet — center of the circle inside the triangle.
